sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=42, col=21).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 13, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'MOUSE': 'Clicking on button horizontal bars (row 42) toggles B↔w. Clicking on yellow shapes, gap, or blue character has no visible effect on shapes (VERIFIED steps 1-10).'}
- Object taxonomy: {'checkerboard': 'B and c alternating 4x4 blocks forming background on white canvas', 'yellow_top': '14px Y shape at rows 13-16, cols 30-33. Solid block with c-colored 2px gap at row 16 cols 31-32', 'yellow_bottom': '16px Y U-shape at rows 33-37, cols 29-34. Open at top, filled bottom row', 'gap_piece': "c-colored 2px piece at [[16,31],[16,32]] in top shape's gap", 'indicator_buttons': '5 toggle buttons at row 42, cols 20-22, 25-27, 30-32, 35-37, 40-42 with vertical stems at rows 44-46', 'blue_character': 'Blue object at bottom bounds [51,32]-[59,40], 69px', 'blue_bar_top': 'Blue bar at row 1, shrinks with each action (timer)'}
- HUD map: {'top_blue_bar': 'Timer/progress bar at row 1'}
Current level:
- World model: Checkerboard with 2 yellow shapes. 5 toggle buttons below board at row 42 (currently B,w,B,w,w). Vertical stems below buttons at rows 44-46 with pattern B,w,B,w,w. Blue character at bottom. Timer bar at top.
- Goal model: Unknown. Buttons toggle B↔w but haven't completed level. Need to find what button combination or interaction completes the puzzle.
- Recent findings: ['Button clicks toggle B↔w (VERIFIED steps 5-8)', 'Yellow shapes never change from any click (VERIFIED steps 1-10)', 'Stem pattern is B,w,B,w,w - matches initial button state', 'Clicking blue character had no visible effect (step 10)']
- Open questions: ['What is the target button pattern?', 'Do vertical stems (rows 44-46) do something different from horizontal bars?', 'Is there a relationship between button states and the yellow shapes?', 'Does the blue character need to interact with something?']
- Plan: Try clicking vertical stems to see if they have different behavior than horizontal button bars
- Strategy log: ['Clicked yellow shapes directly (gap and body) - no effect on shapes. Abandoned direct shape clicking.', "Button toggling: toggled buttons 1 and 3, then toggled back. No level completion. Matching buttons to stem colors didn't work."]
- Failed probes: ['MOUSE(35,31): no change to yellow shapes (step 1)', 'MOUSE(15,31): no change to top U (step 2)', 'MOUSE(16,31): no change to top U (step 3)', 'MOUSE(13,31): no change to top U (step 4)', 'MOUSE(16,31): no change to gap (step 9)', 'MOUSE(55,36): no change to blue character (step 10)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 12 of about 32 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, I can see the buttons now show: w, w, B, w, w (button 1 changed from B to w). And the stems show: w, w, B, w, w. So now all pairs match!
But the level didn't complete. So matching buttons to stems is not the goal.
Let me reconsider the puzzle entirely. I have:
1. Two yellow shapes on a checkerboard
2. 5 toggle buttons with stems (now all matching: w, w, B, w, w)
3. A blue character at the bottom
What else could the goal be? Let me think about the yellow shapes:
- Top shape: A 4x4 block with a 2px gap at the bottom (like an inverted U or crown)
- Bottom shape: A U-shape (open at top, filled bottom)
These shapes are complementary! The top shape has a gap at the bottom, the bottom shape has a gap at the top.
What if the goal is to make the shapes match or combine somehow? But clicking on shapes doesn't change them...
Wait, maybe the buttons DO affect the shapes, but I need to click the buttons first and then click on the shapes? Like the buttons are "tools" or "modes" that determine what happens when you click elsewhere?
Let me try: click a button, then click on a shape.
Or maybe I need to think about this differently. What if the 5 buttons represent 5 different colors or operations, and I need to select the right one before clicking on the shapes?
Let me try: toggle button 3 (currently B, the only B button), then click on the gap in the top shape.
Actually, let me first check the current state more carefully.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# All pairs now match: w,w,B,w,w. Level didn't complete.
# Let me check current state and try a new approach: buttons as "modes" before clicking shapes

ascii = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"Row 42 (buttons): '{ascii[42][18:45]}'")
print(f"Row 44 (stems):    '{ascii[44][18:45]}'")

# New hypothesis: buttons are modes/tools. Click button first, then click shape.
# Let me try: toggle button 3 (currently B) to w, then click the gap
# Or try: keep button 3 as B (unique), then click gap

# Let me try clicking the gap while button 3 is the only B
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 31}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE": "Clicking button bars (row 42) or stems (rows 44-46) toggles B↔w independently. Clicking shapes/gap/blue character has no direct effect."}, "object_taxonomy": {"yellow_top": "14px Y shape with c-gap at row 16 cols 31-32", "yellow_bottom": "16px Y U-shape", "indicator_buttons": "5 toggle buttons at row 42 with stems at rows 44-46"}, "hud_map": {"top_blue_bar": "Timer bar"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "All button-stem pairs now match: w,w,B,w,w. Level not completed. Matching is not the goal.", "goal_model": "Buttons may be 'modes' - select button then click shape", "recent_findings": ["Matching all button-stem pairs did not complete level"], "open_questions": ["Do buttons act as modes for subsequent clicks?"], "current_plan": "Try clicking gap while button 3 is the unique B button", "strategy_log": ["Shape clicking: no effect", "Button toggling: no level completion", "Stem toggling: stems independently toggleable", "Matching pairs: no completion"], "failed_probes": ["MOUSE(35,31)", "MOUSE(15,31)", "MOUSE(16,31)", "MOUSE(13,31)", "MOUSE(16,31) gap", "MOUSE(55,36) blue char", "All pairs matching w,w,B,w,w"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
